name: Moko
description: Moko, in Māori mythology, typically refers to a lizard or reptile, often depicted as a guardian spirit or a messenger from the spirit world. These creatures are not inherently malevolent, but rather represent a connection to the natural world and the ancestral realm. Depending on the specific iwi (tribe) and region, the characteristics and significance of Moko can vary. Some stories portray them as protectors of specific locations, such as sacred groves or waterways. Others depict them as omens, their appearance foretelling good fortune or impending danger. Moko are often associated with mana, the spiritual power and prestige that is central to Māori culture. Their presence can indicate the presence of this power or serve as a reminder of the importance of respecting the natural world. While not always directly involved in major mythological narratives, Moko play a crucial role in the everyday beliefs and practices of Māori communities, embodying the interconnectedness of the physical and spiritual realms and the enduring presence of ancestors.
subtitle: Guardian spirits of the land
appearance: Moko are typically depicted as lizards or reptiles of varying sizes. Their scales can range in color from green and brown to vibrant hues, often reflecting their environment. Some depictions include intricate patterns or markings that symbolize their connection to specific iwi or locations. They may possess piercing eyes that seem to hold ancient wisdom, and their movements can be both swift and stealthy.
habitat: Moko can be found in a variety of natural habitats, including forests, mountains, waterways, and even dwelling places. They are often associated with areas of spiritual significance, such as sacred groves, burial grounds, or places where important events have occurred.
behavior: Moko are generally solitary creatures, preferring to observe and protect their territory from a distance. They are not inherently aggressive, but will defend themselves if threatened. Their behavior can often be interpreted as a sign or omen, with specific actions or appearances indicating different meanings. They are seen as guardians and messengers.

keyMyths.title: Moko as Guardians
keyMyths.description: Moko are often revered as kaitiaki (guardians) of specific places or people, protecting them from harm and ensuring the continued prosperity of the land. They embody the connection between the living and the ancestral realms.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell
sources.work: The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Māori mythology and folklore.
